exemplar.title: An experiment to investigate the Stroop effect in a student population

detailedExemplarData.title: An experiment to investigate the Stroop effect in a student population

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and relevant aim linked to selectiveâattention and speedâofâprocessing theories
Accurate description of Stroop effect and theoretical models
Explicit null and directional hypotheses with correctly identified IV and DV
weaknesses: Minor citation gaps and slight phrasing issues in theory description
Operationalisation details (e.g., stopwatch accuracy) are not fully specified
Link to classroom context could be more deeply contextualized
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although all three introduction strands were assessed in the top band, minor wording and detail gaps (citation omissions, operationalisation specificity) prevent a full 5â6 score. A score of 4 reflects strong performance with room for more precise detail.

strengths: Wellâjustified repeatedâmeasures design controlling participant variables
Opportunity sampling method described and linked to practicality
Controlled variables and materials named with rationale for counterbalancing
weaknesses: Omission of critical stimulus timing and display refresh details
Overstatement of representativeness in opportunity sampling
Lack of demographic depth (e.g., reading proficiency)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All exploration strands were explained, placing them in the top band, but procedural and sampling detail omissions warrant a midâband score of 3 rather than the full 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of mean/SD and Wilcoxon signedârank test with correct significance interpretation
Findings explicitly linked to hypothesis and IV/DV differences
Sound discussion of dispersion and outliers in SD analysis
weaknesses: Graph lacks error bars and is low resolution
Statistical phrasing contains a minor ambiguous comparison
Effect size and exact pâvalue are not reported
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Descriptive and inferential statistics are largely accurate and findings are interpreted against the hypothesis. Graphical and reporting flaws (missing error bars, effect size) keep this in the 3â4 band, meriting a 4.

strengths: Findings tied back to selectiveâattention and speedâofâprocessing theories
Strengths and limitations are clearly explained and linked to validity/reliability
Modifications are justified and explicitly address identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion of findings is somewhat brief and occasionally anecdotal
Quantification of practiceâeffect limitation is not provided
Modification details (e.g., matching variables) lack specificity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation demonstrates strong theoretical integration and justified improvements, but brevity in findings discussion and lack of quantification in limitations prevent a full 5â6 score, resulting in a 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with labelled axes and title is supplied, directly comparing means of the two conditions. While it correctly addresses the hypothesis, there are no error bars and resolution is lowâsmall presentation flaws that warrant 3-4 rather than full marks.

The student states the statistical outcome (W=0, p<.05) and explicitly links it to rejecting the null and accepting the research hypothesis. Interpretation refers back to IV/DV differences, fulfilling the top-band descriptor, though depth is not extensive enough for 6.

Both null and directional hypotheses are provided; IV (word-colour congruency) and DV (completion time in seconds) are identified and operationalised. Terminology is accurate, meeting the 5â6 band. Minor wording issues (e.g., âfaster timeâ) preclude the very top mark.

Opportunity sampling is described and explicitly justified as convenient and broadly representative; exclusion criteria and demographic details are supplied, demonstrating an explanation of participant choice.

The Stroop effect, selective-attention theory and speed-of-processing theory are all described with sufficient psychological detail, and the student explicitly links these theories to the IV manipulation (congruent vs incongruent wordâcolour). A few citation gaps and slight simplifications keep it from perfection, but the link is clearly explained.

Findings are described and connected to selective-attention and speed-of-processing theories, demonstrating understanding, but discussion is relatively brief and occasionally anecdotal, resulting in a solid but not outstanding 3-4 band placement.

The aim is clearly stated â to investigate colourânaming under timed conditions in Year-12 students â and the student explicitly explains why this is relevant to multitasking demands on that population. The explanation links the classroom context to selective-attention limitations, satisfying the top-band descriptor, though minor phrasing weaknesses prevent the full 6 marks.

Multiple strengths (e.g., repeated-measures controls) and limitations (practice effects, limited generalisability) are explained in relation to how they impact validity/reliability, satisfying the highest band, though the analysis is not exhaustive enough for a 6.

Controlled variables (sleep, environment, counterbalancing) and materials (website with timer, answer card, laptop brightness) are named and the rationale for each is stated, constituting clear explanation rather than mere listing.

Proposed modifications (matched-pairs design, broader school sampling, additional neutral stimulus) are explicitly tied to identified limitations and justified in terms of how they would improve validity/generalisation, meeting the 5â6 descriptor; minor detail gaps keep it at 5.

The student not only describes but explains why a repeated-measures design was chosen (controls participant variables, time-efficient) and notes practice-effect risk. This moves beyond description to explanation, fulfilling the upper band for this strand.

Appropriate descriptive (mean/SD) and inferential (Wilcoxon for non-parametric paired data) statistics are used, calculations appear consistent with raw data, and significance is correctly interpreted. A minor mis-statement in the descriptive discussion prevents a 6.

annotations.comment: The proposed neutralâstimulus modification addresses demand characteristics; include specifics on stimulus design and presentation.
The title clearly specifies the phenomenon (Stroop effect) and target population, providing an immediate context for the investigation.
The definition of the Stroop effect is concise and accurate, demonstrating clear understanding of interference between congruent and incongruent stimuli.
The replicated study description ends abruptly; include the full conclusion of Stroopâs original Experiment 3 to strengthen the historical context.
The aim statement is clearly articulated and its relevance to selectiveâattention and speedâofâprocessing theories is well explained.
Both the null and directional hypotheses are explicitly stated and linked to the independent and dependent variables.
The operationalisation description could be improved by specifying how completion time was measured (e.g., stopwatch accuracy, trial count).
The explanation of the repeatedâmeasures design goes beyond description, justifying control of participant variables and efficiency advantages.
The claim of representativeness via opportunity sampling is overstated; consider potential location biases during free periods.
Provide additional demographic details (e.g., cultural background, reading proficiency) to justify the choice of Year 12 students further.
Counterbalancing via random coin flips is wellâjustified to minimize order effects and participant awareness.
Material description omits details on stimulus presentation timing and display refresh rate, which are critical for replicability.
Procedure steps are thorough; consider adding interâtrial interval details to ensure consistent timing between conditions.
The selection of the mean as the measure of central tendency is appropriate given ratioâlevel time data.
The expression â50 < 77.24%â is ambiguous; rephrase this comparison to clarify the percentage difference between means.
The discussion of standard deviations acknowledges outliers and reliability, demonstrating sound interpretation of dispersion.
The summary table needs clear unit labels and consistent significant figures to improve readability and precision.
Add error bars and improve resolution in the bar graph to fully address variability and support the hypothesis visually.
The use of a Wilcoxon signedârank test is well justified given the small sample size and nonparametric requirements.
Report the exact pâvalue and include an effect size (e.g., r or Cohenâs d) to provide a more comprehensive inferential analysis.
The discussion effectively links findings back to selectiveâattention and speedâofâprocessing theories, demonstrating strong theoretical integration.
The evaluation articulates methodological strengths of the repeatedâmeasures design, reinforcing validity considerations.
Practice effects are identified but quantification (e.g., statistical comparison between groups) would strengthen the limitation analysis.
The matchedâpairs design proposal is appropriate; specify the matching variables and pairing procedure to justify selection.
The discussion of opportunity sampling efficiency demonstrates clear understanding of practical constraints.
The generalisability limitation is valid; consider discussing how demographic or educational differences across schools might influence Stroop performance.
The conclusion restates the findings but would benefit from summarizing effect sizes and deeper theoretical implications.
Reference entries lack consistent formatting; ensure all citations follow a single style guide (e.g., APA 7th edition) for uniformity.
Include access dates, DOIs, or retrieval information for online sources to enhance citation completeness and traceability.
